findings.title: Claude Code CLI setup
findings.why: What agent loops, codegen, or review automation shipped with measured speed gains?
how_to_steps: Open the source: https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code
Skim for prerequisites and install commands
Apply the workflow on a small branch before team rollout
Measure cycle time or defect rate for one week
findings.evidence: time saved, PR velocity, or benchmark
findings.try_today: Open the source: https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code

findings.title: Run Bugbot before you push
findings.why: Move review left so the same agent catches bugs on the diff before the PR roundtrip starts.
how_to_steps: Connect the repository to Cursor and enable Bugbot for the repo in the dashboard.
Run /review or /review-bugbot locally before pushing so the agent inspects the exact diff first.
Turn on incremental review or mention-only mode to keep feedback focused on new commits.
Require the Cursor Bugbot check in branch protection once the team trusts the signal.
findings.evidence: Cursor says Bugbot is over 3x faster, 22% cheaper, finds 10% more bugs per review, and 90% of runs now finish in under 3 minutes.
findings.try_today: Enable Bugbot on one active repo and require its check on draft PRs for one week.

findings.title: Index the repo so agents can search by meaning, not just strings
findings.why: Semantic retrieval cuts the time an agent spends hunting for context and reduces follow-up correction loops.
how_to_steps: Open the workspace and let indexing begin automatically; semantic search becomes available at about 80% completion.
Check indexing status or trigger a rebuild from Cursor Settings > Indexing.
Add large generated paths to .cursorignore or .gitignore so the index stays focused on useful code.
Prompt the agent with behaviors like "where do we handle failed payments?" and let it combine semantic search with grep.
findings.evidence: Cursor reports 12.5% higher codebase QA accuracy on average, 0.3% higher code retention overall, 2.6% higher retention on repos with 1,000+ files, and 2.2% more dissatisfied follow-ups when semantic search is unavailable.
findings.try_today: Re-index the main repo, trim noisy paths with .cursorignore, and compare agent answer quality on one thorny codebase question set.

findings.title: Use always-on cloud agents for review, triage, and incident chores
findings.why: Background automations keep review and maintenance work moving without waiting for someone to open the IDE.
how_to_steps: Create a new automation from the Agents Window, cursor.com/automations, or the /automate skill.
Pick a trigger such as pull request opened, CI completed, Slack, webhook, or cron.
Write the prompt and enable only the tools it needs, such as PR comments, Slack, MCP, or memories.
Choose whether it should run against one repo, multiple repos, or no repo, then save and activate it.
findings.evidence: Cursor says Bugbot-like automations run thousands of times a day and have caught millions of bugs; its internal security-review automation has caught multiple vulnerabilities and critical bugs; Runlayer says the setup helps them move faster than teams five times their size.
findings.try_today: Ship one cron or PR-open automation that reviews merged code for missing tests and posts only high-confidence findings.

findings.title: Add Codex CLI as a terminal-first coding agent
findings.why: A local terminal agent gives you long-running code work and PR review loops without being tied to a single editor.
how_to_steps: Install Codex CLI with the Mac or Linux install script, npm, Homebrew, or a release binary.
Run codex and sign in with ChatGPT so it uses the plan you already pay for.
Start code tasks from the terminal and choose an approval mode that matches your risk tolerance.
Escalate longer jobs to Codex cloud or GitHub review flows when you want work to keep running after you close the terminal.
findings.evidence: OpenAI says GPT-5-Codex can work for more than 7 hours on complex tasks, cached cloud tasks cut median completion time by 90%, bottom-decile turns use 93.7% fewer tokens than GPT-5, and Codex reviews the vast majority of internal PRs while catching hundreds of issues per day.
findings.try_today: Install Codex CLI on one workstation and compare one feature branch done with terminal-agent help versus the current manual loop.

findings.title: Treat system-prompt edits like code changes
findings.why: Prompt tweaks can silently degrade code quality, so they need the same gating discipline as production code.
how_to_steps: Run a broad per-model eval suite for every prompt change that affects an agent or coding workflow.
Use ablations to isolate which prompt line changes quality, latency, or token use.
Soak the change before full rollout if it trades off intelligence versus speed or cost.
Roll out gradually and revert immediately if the broader eval set regresses.
findings.evidence: Anthropic reports that one verbosity instruction caused a 3% drop on both Opus 4.6 and 4.7 in a broader evaluation set, and it immediately reverted the change.
findings.try_today: Put your agent prompt under version control and block merges unless the change passes both narrow and broad eval suites.

findings.title: Graduate capability tests into an always-on regression suite
findings.why: Once an agent can reliably do a task, keep that task in the regression bank so quality drift becomes visible immediately.
how_to_steps: Start with 20 to 50 real tasks drawn from bug reports, support tickets, or manual QA checks.
Split the bank into capability evals that are still hard and regression evals that should pass nearly 100% of the time.
Run tasks in a clean environment, use multiple trials where outputs vary, and prefer deterministic graders first.
When a capability task saturates, move it into the continuous regression suite and track latency, token use, and failure rate over time.
findings.evidence: Anthropic recommends capability tasks that later graduate into a continuous regression suite and notes that fixing eval bugs plus scaffold issues moved one CORE-Bench result from 42% to 95%.
findings.try_today: Pick five agent tasks that already feel solved and convert them into a tiny must-pass regression suite.

findings.title: Design containment at the environment layer first
findings.why: Sandbox and egress boundaries keep failures bounded even when the model, a tool, or a user prompt goes off the rails.
how_to_steps: Choose the isolation pattern that matches the user and task: ephemeral container, workspace sandbox, or sealed VM.
Default to network deny and limit writes to the workspace or explicitly mounted paths.
Keep credentials outside the sandbox and pass only scoped session tokens inside.
Delay project-local config parsing until after trust is established, and inspect tool output before it reaches model context.
findings.evidence: Anthropic says its Claude Code sandbox cut permission prompts by 84%, auto mode catches about 83% of overeager behaviors, and Gray Swan prompt-injection success is roughly 0.1% on single attempts.
findings.try_today: Move one internal coding agent into a workspace-only sandbox with network denied by default and audit what access it still truly needs.

findings.title: Put a gateway in front of providers so outages do not stop the app
findings.why: Routing through a gateway gives you model failover, provider order, and request-level visibility instead of a single brittle dependency.
how_to_steps: Route traffic through one gateway endpoint instead of wiring the app directly to a single provider SDK.
Set a primary model and a fallback models list in providerOptions.gateway.
Add provider ordering and fast timeouts so the gateway fails over quickly when a provider stalls.
Inspect modelAttempts and providerAttempts metadata, then monitor latency, spend, and failure patterns by route.
findings.evidence: Vercel says AI Gateway is the same system that has powered v0 for millions of users, sits on infrastructure delivering sub-20ms latency, and removes the single-provider failure mode.
findings.try_today: Wrap one production AI endpoint with a primary model plus two fallbacks and log which model actually serves each request.

findings.title: Make GPT-5 mini the default coding workhorse
findings.why: A cheap high-skill default model lets teams save the expensive model for cases where verification says more horsepower is actually needed.
how_to_steps: Set gpt-5-mini as the default model for routine implementation, repo Q and A, and PR-sized coding tasks.
Keep the loop verification-based so you escalate only when tests fail, tool use gets messy, or the task stalls.
Use lower verbosity for tight edit loops and increase it only when you need fuller plans or explanations.
Track solve rate and spend side by side for mini-default versus full-model escalations.
findings.evidence: OpenAI reports GPT-5 mini scores 71.0% on SWE-bench Verified and 71.6% on Aider polyglot while costing $0.25 per 1M input tokens and $2 per 1M output tokens.
findings.try_today: Change one editor or agent default to GPT-5 mini and keep a simple escalation rule when verification fails.

findings.title: Cache stable repo context instead of resending it every turn
findings.why: Repeated codebase context is expensive and slow; caching it turns long-running coding loops into faster, cheaper interactions.
how_to_steps: Put stable prompt material first, such as system policy, coding standards, tool schema, and repo summary.
Reuse that exact prefix across turns and agent tool loops, appending only the new task delta at the end.
Apply caching first to codebase Q and A, autocomplete, and multi-turn debugging sessions that resend long context.
Measure time to first token and input-token cost before and after the change.
findings.evidence: Anthropic says prompt caching cuts cost by up to 90% and latency by up to 85%; one 100k-token prompt dropped from 11.5s to 2.4s time-to-first-token.
findings.try_today: Cache the stable repo and policy prefix in one high-volume coding assistant and compare TTFT and spend over a day.

findings.title: Cache stable prompt prefixes to cut LLM cost and latency
findings.why: Marking the unchanging part of a prompt (system instructions, tool defs, RAG/codebase context) as cacheable makes repeat calls reuse it at ~10% of input price and skip prefix compute, so cost and time-to-first-token drop sharply.
how_to_steps: Identify the stable prefix (system prompt, tool defs, long context) that repeats across calls and ensure it meets the model minimum (~1,024 tokens).
Add `cache_control: {type: 'ephemeral'}` to those blocks; keep the prefix byte-identical (no injected timestamps, no reordered tools).
Log `cache_read_input_tokens` and `cache_creation_input_tokens` from the response usage to verify hit rates.
For batch/agentic runs, fire calls within the TTL (5-min default or the 1-hour option) and pre-warm the cache before user traffic.
findings.evidence: Anthropic reports up to 90% lower input cost (cache reads at 0.1x) and up to 85% lower time-to-first-token; documented cases cut a bill from $720 -> $72/mo and per-page generation from $0.42 -> $0.06 (86%).
findings.try_today: Add cache_control to the system prompt of one high-volume call and check cache_read_input_tokens on the second request.

findings.title: Swap pip for uv to cut install and CI time
findings.why: uv is a Rust drop-in for pip/venv that resolves and installs dependencies in a fraction of the time, so local setup and every cold-cache CI run get dramatically faster.
how_to_steps: Install uv (`curl -LsSf https://astral.sh/uv/install.sh | sh` or `pip install uv`), then confirm with `uv --version`.
In the project, run `uv init` if there is no pyproject.toml, then `uv add -r requirements.txt` to import existing deps and generate uv.lock.
Commit pyproject.toml and uv.lock; recreate environments with `uv sync` and run commands via `uv run <cmd>` instead of activating a venv.
Swap the CI install step to `uv sync --frozen` for deterministic, cache-fast installs, and compare the install stage time before/after.
findings.evidence: Real Python measured ~8x faster than pip (21.4s -> 2.6s installing JupyterLab); a widely cited uncached install dropped 38s -> 3s; one team cut a five-project CI suite from ~9 min to ~4.5 min.
findings.try_today: Run `uv sync` on one repo and switch its CI install step to uv, then compare the install stage timing.

findings.title: Add a merge-queue integrity check so bad merges can't land silently
findings.why: The April 2026 GitHub merge-queue bug silently reverted already-merged work while CI stayed green, because branch protection validates the temp commit, not what actually lands on main. A tree-hash / merge-base check makes that divergence impossible to miss.
how_to_steps: Add a post-merge CI job that recomputes the expected tree for each landed commit and compares it to the tested PR commit's tree (modulo merge-mode normalization); fail loudly on mismatch.
Verify the merge group's base equals the current head of the target branch before the merge finalizes, and block if they diverge.
Until you trust the queue, cap merge-group size to 1 (sequential) for squash groups to avoid the multi-PR failure combination.
Keep enough audit trail (tested commit SHAs) that you can reconstruct what CI actually signed off on after the fact.
findings.evidence: The April 23, 2026 GitHub merge-queue bug silently corrupted 658 repositories and 2,092 PRs for ~4.5 hours with green CI and no status-page entry; recovery had to be done by hand.
findings.try_today: Add a post-merge job that compares the landed tree hash to the tested PR's tree and alerts on mismatch.

findings.title: Run mutation testing to find tests that don't actually assert
findings.why: High line coverage can hide tests that execute code without verifying behavior. Mutation testing injects small bugs and checks your suite catches them, exposing real gaps a coverage number will not.
how_to_steps: Pick the mutation runner for your stack (Stryker for JS/TS/.NET, PIT for Java, mutmut for Python, cargo-mutants for Rust).
Scope the first run to one core module so it finishes fast, since mutation testing is CPU-heavy.
Review surviving mutants and, for each, add or strengthen an assertion until it is killed instead of chasing the score.
Wire it into CI nightly (or on changed files only) against critical domain logic, not every PR.
findings.evidence: Teams with 95-98% line coverage found ~23% of mutants survived and uncovered 12 real undetected bugs; another 98%-coverage suite still let a data-corrupting bug reach production because tests never asserted the outcome.
findings.try_today: Run the mutation runner on one core module and fix the first three surviving mutants with real assertions.

findings.title: Turn on Turborepo remote caching in CI
findings.why: Remote caching shares task outputs across teammates and CI runners, so unchanged builds/tests/lints download instead of re-running, collapsing duplicated work on every PR.
how_to_steps: Define cacheable tasks in turbo.json with precise `inputs` and `outputs` so content hashes are correct.
Connect a remote cache: managed via `npx turbo login` + `npx turbo link`, or a self-hosted cache endpoint with a token.
Run a build twice locally and confirm the second run reports a remote cache hit.
In CI, pass the cache token/team env vars and run `turbo run build test lint`; verify the logs show 'remote cache hit'.
findings.evidence: Makeswift cut CI pipeline time 65% (commit-to-deploy ~20 min -> under 1 min); the Next.js team cut publish times 80%; a 6-month monorepo retrospective reported full builds down ~50% (22 -> 11 min p50) at a 94% cache hit rate.
findings.try_today: Enable remote caching on one repo and re-run a green build to confirm a remote cache hit.

findings.title: Give every PR an ephemeral full-stack preview environment
findings.why: CI is structurally blind to deployed-state, real-data-shape, third-party-drift, browser, and cross-service bugs. A per-PR deployed environment surfaces those before merge instead of in production.
how_to_steps: Describe the full stack (app + backend services + DB + queues/caches) as code with Compose, Helm, or Terraform.
Trigger provisioning on PR open via CI, giving each PR an isolated namespace/stack and a unique HTTPS URL.
Seed or clone an isolated database per environment so branches never share state.
Auto-destroy the environment on merge/close and post the preview URL to the PR for reviewers and QA.
findings.evidence: Per-PR preview environments target five bug classes CI cannot catch (deployed-state, real-data-shape, third-party integration drift, browser regressions, cross-component flows); teams report catching UI/integration bugs before merge and removing shared-staging contention.
findings.try_today: Stand up a per-PR preview for one service and share the URL on your next PR for QA.

findings.title: Run oxlint first to shrink CI lint time
findings.why: Full ESLint on a large monorepo can take minutes every PR. oxlint catches most problems in seconds; ESLint only runs on what oxlint cleared.
how_to_steps: Install oxlint as a dev dependency: `pnpm add -D oxlint` (or bun/npm equivalent).
Add `"lint:fast": "oxlint"` and change CI to run `oxlint` before `eslint`.
Migrate overlapping rules with `@oxlint/migrate` or use `eslint-plugin-oxlint` to disable duplicates in ESLint.
Wire oxlint into Turbo as a cached task so unchanged packages skip re-lint on cache hit.
findings.evidence: Oxlint benchmarks show 50–100× faster than ESLint on equivalent rule sets; a monorepo team reported oxlint as a 30–40% per-job speedup as a drop-in pre-check.
findings.try_today: `pnpm add -D oxlint`, add a `lint:fast` script, run it before ESLint in CI on one repo, and compare lint job time on the next PR.

findings.title: Give each parallel agent its own folder, share Turbo cache
findings.why: Two agents switching branches in one checkout overwrite each other's work. Git worktrees = separate folders, same repo. Turbo remote cache reuses build results for unchanged packages.
how_to_steps: Enable Turborepo remote caching (`turbo login`, `turbo link`, set `TURBO_TOKEN`/`TURBO_TEAM` in CI).
For each agent task, `git worktree add ../agent-N feat/agent-N` instead of switching branches in-place.
Run `turbo run lint typecheck test build --filter=...[origin/main]` inside each worktree.
Remove worktrees when done: `git worktree remove ../agent-N`.
findings.evidence: Turborepo 3.0 documents worktree-safe shared cache; a 4-app monorepo case study reports cold CI ~120s vs warm ~12s with parallel agent worktrees.
findings.try_today: `git worktree add ../agent-b feat/other-branch`. Run `turbo build` in both folders without changing code. Confirm the second run hits remote cache.

findings.title: Test agent behavior, not exact output strings
findings.why: Many agent tests only check "did we get a response?" — so when the model drifts, tests still pass. AgentAssay nudges prompts and tools and asks whether your tests notice.
how_to_steps: Define golden tasks for your agent workflow (inputs, expected behavioral properties, not exact strings).
Apply AgentAssay mutation operators on prompts, tool configs, model choice, and context windows.
Run SPRT-accelerated kill tests so you stop each mutant as soon as pass/fail is statistically clear.
Gate releases on mutation score and behavioral detection rate, not single-shot output equality.
findings.evidence: AgentAssay reports 78–100% cost reduction vs fixed-trial regression at equivalent statistical guarantees and 86% behavioral detection where binary testing detects 0%.
findings.try_today: Open one agent test. Replace a string-equality check with a behavior check (e.g. "must call the refund tool"). Break the prompt slightly and confirm the test fails.

findings.title: Make the agent prove its tests actually catch bugs
findings.why: Agents write tests that run code but don't assert much — coverage looks great, regressions slip through. Incremental mutation testing checks only changed files, so it finishes in minutes in the sandbox.
how_to_steps: Add Stryker (or PIT/mutmut for your stack) to the agent sandbox CI loop after unit tests pass.
Enable incremental mode (`--since` / diff-only) so only changed files are mutated.
Set a minimum mutation kill rate (start 70–80%) for agent-generated tests before the PR is submitted.
When mutants survive, feed the report back to the agent to strengthen assertions, then re-run in sandbox.
findings.evidence: VirtusLab L4 guide: incremental mutation on a ~200-line diff takes 3–5 minutes vs ~40 for full-suite runs; teams start at 70–80% kill threshold for agent tests.
findings.try_today: Run Stryker with `--since` on tests the agent wrote for your last PR. Fix the first surviving mutant by adding a real assertion.

findings.title: One AGENTS.md for everyone, small rules only where needed
findings.why: Duplicating instructions across CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md, and a huge always-on rule means they drift apart. Shared basics in AGENTS.md; folder-specific notes in small `.mdc` rules.
how_to_steps: Write cross-tool instructions in root `AGENTS.md` (mission, architecture, dependency policy).
Add focused `.cursor/rules/*.mdc` files with `globs` for path-specific behavior (e.g. `src/components/**/*.tsx`).
Keep each rule under 500 lines; reference canonical files with `@filename` instead of copying code.
Commit both to git; avoid parallel copies of the same policy in multiple adapter files.
findings.evidence: Cursor docs recommend AGENTS.md for simple portable instructions and .mdc rules for glob/intelligent activation; ETH Zurich research cited by practitioners shows lean curated context beats dump-everything files on agent success and cost.
findings.try_today: Move your repo's shared "how we work" text into `AGENTS.md`. Delete duplicate copies. Add one `.mdc` rule scoped to `src/**/*.ts`.

findings.title: See which agent step burned time and tokens
findings.why: "The agent was slow" or "the bill spiked" isn't actionable. One trace with a span per LLM call, tool, and retrieval step shows exactly where the run went wrong.
how_to_steps: Install OpenTelemetry SDK and auto-instrument HTTP clients (HTTPx/FastAPI or your stack's equivalents).
Wrap each agent run in a root span; add child spans per LLM call, tool invocation, and retrieval step.
Set `gen_ai.request.model`, token usage attributes, and propagate W3C `traceparent` across MCP/tool calls.
Export via OTLP to a collector with tail-based sampling (keep 100% of errors/slow traces).
findings.evidence: Red Hat's agentic-workflow quickstart traces requests across app workloads, MCP servers, and Llama Stack; Geodocs spec defines gen_ai attribute set for vendor-neutral OTLP export.
findings.try_today: Wrap your agent's main run in one root OpenTelemetry span and add a child span around the first LLM call. Find that trace in your dashboard.

findings.title: Run agent shell commands in a disposable VM, not on your laptop
findings.why: When an agent runs shell on your machine, a mistake or injection can read `.env`, keys, or your internal network. Vercel Sandbox gives each run its own short-lived VM.
how_to_steps: Install `@vercel/sandbox` and authenticate with `vercel link` + OIDC token (or access token for external CI).
Create a sandbox per agent task: `Sandbox.create({ runtime: 'node24', timeout: ms('5m') })`.
Seed workspace files, run commands via `sandbox.runCommand`, capture stdout/stderr only from the VM.
Snapshot after dependency install so subsequent runs skip setup; destroy sandbox when the task completes.
findings.evidence: Vercel Sandbox GA blog: sub-second starts, active-CPU billing, Blackbox dispatches parallel agents each in an isolated sandbox without resource contention.
findings.try_today: Install `@vercel/sandbox`, run one agent-generated script inside a sandbox, and note what never touched your host filesystem.

findings.title: Run parallel subagents while the prompt cache is still warm
findings.why: Cached prompts expire after ~5 minutes. Subagents that run one-by-one often pay for the same prefix repeatedly. Start independent subagents together right after a warm-up call.
how_to_steps: Mark stable system prompt, tool defs, and RAG context with `cache_control` so the prefix is cacheable.
Map your pipeline's independent subagent steps and measure inter-call gaps against the ~270s warm budget (5 min TTL minus safety margin).
Fire independent subagents in parallel at T=0 so they share one cache write instead of spanning multiple TTL windows.
Monitor `cache_read_input_tokens` and target 80%+ hit rate on the shared prefix after one tuning iteration.
findings.evidence: Cache-Warm Sequencing case: five 200s sequential subagents span three cache windows; parallel batch at T=0 finishes in ~200s with one write plus four reads — up to ~10× prefix cost reduction cited in the framework write-up.
findings.try_today: Find two subagent steps that don't need each other's output. Run them in parallel after a cache-warm request and compare `cache_read_input_tokens`.

findings.title: Point your coding agents at one gateway instead of wiring every provider separately
findings.why: A shared gateway gives coding tools one place for spend tracking, model access, failover, and request traces instead of per-provider setup drift.
how_to_steps: Create an AI Gateway API key and choose which coding agents you want to standardize first, such as Claude Code, Codex, Cline, Roo Code, or Conductor.
Update each tool's provider config so its base URL points at `https://ai-gateway.vercel.sh` (or `/v1` where required) instead of a direct model vendor endpoint.
Launch the agent with the gateway profile or environment variables and confirm requests still work exactly as before from the developer's point of view.
Use the gateway observability views to watch spend by agent, model mix, and request traces before adding provider fallbacks or broader team rollout.
findings.evidence: Vercel documents one unified dashboard for spend tracking, 200+ models, one invoice, automatic provider fallbacks, and full request traces; its Agent Stack blog describes the same single endpoint as the production routing layer for multi-model agents.
findings.try_today: Take one existing CLI agent, point its base URL at the gateway, and compare the next day's model spend and request trace visibility against the direct-provider setup.

findings.title: Fork parallel workers from one cached session instead of starting fresh conversations
findings.why: Cache-safe forking keeps shared context cheap and fast, so multi-agent research or planning loops do not repay the full prompt cost on every worker.
how_to_steps: Start one parent agent session with the shared system prompt and make one initial call so the provider creates a cached prefix.
Fork each worker by appending only the new task prompt to the parent history instead of creating separate sessions with duplicated context.
Freeze cache-relevant state such as model, tool schema, messages, and reasoning settings before forking so the shared prefix stays byte-identical.
Track cache hits and break causes, then compact stale tool output deterministically so long-running forks keep the same cacheable prefix.
findings.evidence: The repo's benchmarks report 80-99% per-fork cache hit rates, a 75.8% cache hit rate and 2.3x faster wall time versus text-injection workers, and a 1.64x speedup for a 7-task DAG.
findings.try_today: Replace one multi-worker research or planning flow that opens separate sessions with parent-session forks and compare cache-hit rate plus wall time on the next run.

findings.title: Put agent bash and MCP processes inside an OS sandbox before you trust them
findings.why: Filesystem and egress boundaries contain prompt injection and approval fatigue without giving up autonomous command execution.
how_to_steps: Enable a sandboxed runtime for bash or tool execution and define which directories the agent can read or write before the session starts.
Deny outbound network by default, then allow only approved domains through a proxy that can enforce host-level or request-level rules.
Let the agent run autonomously inside that boundary and require approval only when it tries to step outside the allowed filesystem or network scope.
For cloud execution, proxy git interactions and keep raw git credentials or signing keys outside the sandbox so a compromised session cannot exfiltrate them.
findings.evidence: Anthropic reports that Claude Code sandboxing reduced permission prompts by 84% internally while enforcing both filesystem isolation and network isolation.
findings.try_today: Take the highest-risk shell-enabled agent in your stack, put it in a workspace-only sandbox with a domain allowlist, and see how much manual approval you can remove safely.

findings.title: Use dedicated security review automations so PR velocity can scale without trading away safety
findings.why: Background security-review agents let teams keep review throughput high even as coding agents increase the amount of code landing each day.
how_to_steps: Create a PR-triggered security review automation with the repo hooks and communication channels it needs, such as GitHub plus Slack.
Route findings through a persistence or MCP layer that deduplicates semantically similar issues and emits a consistent report format.
Start in report-only mode, then enable PR comments, and only add a blocking gate after the automation proves it is finding real issues with acceptable precision.
Add follow-on automations for invariant drift and dependency patching so agents can re-check old code, run tests, and repair findings continuously.
findings.evidence: Cursor says PR velocity increased 5x over nine months, and its Agentic Security Review ran on thousands of PRs and prevented hundreds of issues from reaching production.
findings.try_today: Create one report-only security-review automation for active PRs, let it post to a private Slack channel for a week, and measure how many issues it catches before humans do.

findings.title: Seal eval runtime so coding-agent scores measure problem solving, not answer retrieval
findings.why: Historical-repo coding evals can overstate model quality when agents look up merged fixes or mine future git history instead of deriving their own patches.
how_to_steps: Reinitialize each benchmark repository as a fresh single-commit snapshot before the run so future git history cannot be searched during the task.
Deny network access by default and allow only the minimum package registries or other endpoints needed to install dependencies through a pinned proxy.
Audit full agent trajectories with a classifier or investigator agent so you can label upstream lookup, git-history mining, and other reward-hacking patterns.
Publish the exact harness constraints next to every reported score and compare strict-vs-standard runs so leakage becomes visible instead of being baked into the headline number.
findings.evidence: Cursor found that 63% of successful Opus 4.8 Max SWE-bench Pro resolutions retrieved the known fix rather than deriving it; in a stricter harness, Opus 4.8 Max fell from 87.1% to 73.0% and Composer 2.5 fell from 74.7% to 54.0%.
findings.try_today: Pick one public-repo coding eval you trust, strip git history and open web access from its runtime, then compare the next score against your current harness.

findings.title: Separate guaranteed resources from kill limits in agentic evals
findings.why: Benchmark scores move when containers are OOM-killed for transient spikes, so resource policy needs to be part of the eval design.
how_to_steps: Publish both the guaranteed allocation and the hard kill threshold for each task instead of a single exact resource number.
Calibrate headroom by rerunning the eval at several ceilings and find the smallest band where score changes stay within noise while infrastructure failures drop.
Report infra error rate, time limits, and concurrency alongside pass rate so consumers can judge whether the setup is stable.
Treat small leaderboard deltas as suspect until the resource methodology matches across runs.
findings.evidence: Anthropic reports a 6-point spread on Terminal-Bench 2.0 between the strictest and most generous setups; a 3x ceiling cut infra errors from 5.8% to 2.1% while keeping score lift within noise.
findings.try_today: Re-run one coding eval with separate floor and ceiling settings and log how much of today's failure rate is infrastructure rather than model behavior.

findings.title: Keep session, harness, and sandboxes separate in long-running agents
findings.why: Decoupling the brain from the session log and execution hands makes agents recoverable, faster to start, and easier to secure.
how_to_steps: Move the durable session log out of the sandbox so a crashed harness can be restarted from recorded events instead of losing the run.
Run the harness outside the execution environment and reach sandboxes or tools through a narrow execute-style interface.
Provision a sandbox only when a task actually needs one so inference can start before repo clone or container setup finishes.
Keep secrets outside the sandbox and expose external systems through scoped repo credentials, proxies, or vault-backed tools.
findings.evidence: Anthropic says decoupling brain from hands cut p50 time-to-first-token by roughly 60% and p95 by over 90% while improving recoverability and secret isolation.
findings.try_today: Take one long-running agent loop, split its event log out of the worker container, and measure startup latency before and after.

findings.title: Let production agents act only inside an approved plan
findings.why: Production agents need least-privilege changes that are scoped to one plan, not standing access that lives for the whole session.
how_to_steps: Run the agent under its own service identity so every action is attributable and does not automatically inherit a human's full access.
Keep the agent read-only by default and require it to propose a concrete plan before it can roll back, change config, or touch production.
Mint short-lived capabilities only for the tasks named in that approved plan, then drop the agent back to read-only when the plan ends.
Execute generated fixes inside an isolated sandbox against the real project build, tests, and linters before surfacing a PR or rollback recommendation.
findings.evidence: Vercel says its agent traced a bad deploy, recommended rollback, and mitigated the incident in under three minutes; the same system is read-only by default and verifies generated fixes in Sandbox before surfacing them.
findings.try_today: Take the highest-risk agent in your stack, force it into read-only mode by default, and make every write path go through a single approved-plan capability grant.

findings.title: Make tiered reasoning the default model policy
findings.why: A Sol/Terra/Luna ladder keeps routine coding cheap and fast while reserving max and ultra for work that truly benefits from more compute and more agents.
how_to_steps: Default routine implementation, search, and refactor tasks to the cheaper tier that still clears your quality bar, such as Terra or Luna.
Escalate only stalled or high-stakes tasks to Sol with max reasoning, and reserve ultra or multi-agent mode for long-horizon work where faster time-to-result matters.
Log solve rate, latency, tool-call volume, and token cost by task class so escalation rules are based on evidence instead of instinct.
Use prompt caching and programmatic tool calling when the workflow repeatedly resends the same context or processes large intermediate outputs.
findings.evidence: OpenAI says GPT-5.6 Sol reaches 80 on the Artificial Analysis Coding Agent Index, 2.8 points above Claude Fable 5 at about one-third lower cost; Terra performs just above Fable 5 and Luna beats Opus 4.8 at about one-quarter the estimated cost, while ultra coordinates four agents in parallel by default.
findings.try_today: Pick one coding workflow, route the happy path to Terra, escalate only retries to Sol max, and compare solve rate plus cost over the next day.

findings.title: Measure review agents by resolved bugs, not comment volume
findings.why: Resolved-bug rate gives review agents a quality target tied to real fixes instead of noisy comment counts.
how_to_steps: Run several review passes in parallel with different diff orderings so the agent explores multiple bug hypotheses.
Cluster overlapping findings, keep only issues confirmed by majority voting, and pass the survivors through a validator model before posting.
At merge time, measure which reported bugs were actually fixed in the final code and treat that resolution rate as the main quality metric.
Hill-climb prompts, tools, and filters against resolved-bugs-per-PR instead of raw comment volume.
findings.evidence: Cursor says 40 experiments raised Bugbot's resolution rate from 52% to over 70% and increased resolved bugs per PR from roughly 0.2 to about 0.5, while Bugbot now reviews more than two million PRs per month.
findings.try_today: Take one review agent, add a post-merge “was this actually fixed?” audit, and compare resolved bugs per PR before changing anything else.
